title: What is the difference between a single-tier and a multi-tiered application in the context of Java's new HTTP/2 client (introduced in Java 11)?

answers.junior: A single-tier application is a monolithic architecture where all components (presentation, business logic, and data access) reside in a single process on one machine, while a multi-tiered application separates these concerns across different layers or services that communicate over a network.
answers.middle: In the context of Java 11's HttpClient, this distinction becomes significant because single-tier applications can use the HttpClient primarily for external integrations or third-party API calls, whereas multi-tiered applications rely heavily on it for inter-service communication between layers (frontend-to-backend, backend-to-database service, microservice-to-microservice). The HttpClient's HTTP/2 support provides substantial benefits for multi-tiered architectures through request multiplexing over a single TCP connection, which reduces latency and improves throughput when making multiple concurrent requests between tiers, whereas single-tier applications see minimal networking overhead benefits since components already share the same process.
answers.senior: For example, in a multi-tiered system, your REST API tier might efficiently make dozens of concurrent HTTP requests to downstream services using HttpClient.newBuilder().version(HttpClient.Version.HTTP_2).build(), while a single-tier monolith would primarily use the HttpClient for external API integrations with less multiplexing benefit. Additionally, multi-tiered applications gain from HTTP/2's server push capabilities and header compression when services frequently exchange data, improving bandwidth efficiency and reducing round-trip times across network boundaries.
answers.tricky: Single-tier architectures don't leverage these HTTP/2 advantages internally but still benefit from modern features like connection pooling and timeout configuration when calling external services. The choice of architecture fundamentally affects how you design network communication patterns and utilize the HttpClient's capabilities—multi-tiered systems should actively leverage HTTP/2's multiplexing for performance-critical inter-service calls, while single-tier applications should focus on reliable external integration patterns.
